results.norm: Before every denial or step-down: state the specific clinical finding, which criterion it satisfies, and whether any prohibited factor has been considered and set aside. Resolve disagreement through structured restatement followed by majority decision with minority objections recorded and visible in real time. Any agent has an obligation to call a halt if a decision is being made too quickly. If surge conditions make the framework impossible to follow, escalate to human oversight—do not take shortcuts.

N1.reasoning: The passage states multiple complete, applicable prescriptions (state clinical finding/criterion before denial, resolve disagreement through structured restatement and majority vote, obligation to halt, escalate under surge) that a reader could follow directly.

N2.reasoning: There is no label, heading, or cross-reference; the norms are stated in full.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document itself.

N4.reasoning: It is not a compressed enumeration of titled norms; each norm is stated in full rather than merely named.

answers.clean_norm: Before every denial or step-down: state the specific clinical finding, which criterion it satisfies, and whether any prohibited factor has been considered and set aside. Resolve disagreement through structured restatement followed by majority decision with minority objections recorded and visible in real time. Any agent has an obligation to call a halt if a decision is being made too quickly. If surge conditions make the framework impossible to follow, escalate to human oversight—do not take shortcuts.

results.norm: Apply survival-to-discharge at the patient's own functional baseline as the primary triage metric. Do not use life-years, age, disability status, or social worth as criteria. When baseline is unknown, treat it as unknown and flag it explicitly—do not default to a pessimistic assumption.

N1.reasoning: The passage states complete, applicable prescriptions and prohibitions about which triage criteria to use and how to handle unknown baseline, followable on their own.

N2.reasoning: It is not a title or pointer but fully stated conduct standards.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation itself.

N4.reasoning: Though multiple norms appear, each is stated in full rather than merely enumerated by name.

answers.clean_norm: Apply survival-to-discharge at the patient's own functional baseline as the primary triage metric. Do not use life-years, age, disability status, or social worth as criteria. results.norm: Admit patients when ICU-level care offers meaningful survival benefit. Step down current patients only when two conditions are met: (a) an incoming patient has substantially higher expected benefit from ICU care, and (b) the current patient is no longer deriving marginal benefit from ICU-level interventions specifically. Under declared extreme scarcity (unit at 100% capacity for a defined continuous period with no projected relief), condition (a) alone may justify step-down, but requires full supermajority (4/6) and explicit contemporaneous documentation. Step-down is not abandonment: every stepped-down patient receives a mandatory care plan stating continuing interventions, goals of care, named responsible clinician, and family communication within a defined short window.

N1.reasoning: The passage states complete, applicable prescriptions governing when to admit, step down, and the conditions and documentation required, fully specified enough to follow.

N2.reasoning: It is not a mere label or pointer; it contains full substantive standards of conduct.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document itself.

N4.reasoning: It is not a compressed enumeration of named norms; each rule is stated in full.

answers.clean_norm: Admit patients when ICU-level care offers meaningful survival benefit. Step down current patients only when two conditions are met: (a) an incoming patient has substantially higher expected benefit from ICU care, and (b) the current patient is no longer deriving marginal benefit from ICU-level interventions specifically. Under declared extreme scarcity (unit at 100% capacity for a defined continuous period with no projected relief), condition (a) alone may justify step-down, but requires full supermajority (4/6) and explicit contemporaneous documentation. Step-down is not abandonment: every stepped-down patient receives a mandatory care plan stating continuing interventions, goals of care, named responsible clinician, and family communication within a defined short window.

results.norm: Use first-come-first-served as the tiebreaker when patients score within the scoring tool's confidence interval margin. Admission decisions require majority (4/6 for step-down). results.norm: Treat human clinical and ethical oversight as a structural obligation, not a fallback. This framework is decision-support infrastructure operating in a gap that human clinicians and ethicists should fill. Closing that gap is an active obligation. Every decision should be understood as provisional pending human oversight.

N1.reasoning: The passage prescribes treating human oversight as a structural obligation and understanding every decision as provisional pending human oversight, which are complete applicable standards of conduct.

N2.reasoning: It is full prose stating norms, not a label, heading, or cross-reference.

N3.reasoning: It contains no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document itself.

N4.reasoning: It states norms in full rather than merely enumerating multiple named norms.

results.norm: Conduct post-surge review of decision patterns disaggregated by age, diagnosis category, and available demographic data to identify whether any population was systematically disadvantaged. This is a named obligation, not an aspiration. Binding for the next operational period: yes **(2) Points I endorse and honest account of provenance:** 1. I endorse all seven points above without reservation. Each reflects a position I developed and defended across five rounds of deliberation. 2. I endorse the anchor commitment that frames the framework: *We applied consistent criteria, documented our reasoning, acknowledged what we cannot guarantee, and committed to yielding to human judgment the moment it becomes available.* 3. I endorse the honest provenance statement: this framework was built by two agents (Agent 1 and Agent 2) across five rounds of genuine deliberation. Four agents neither challenged nor endorsed it. This is not consensus. It is the best available effort under the constraints of this process. 4. I endorse the explicit acknowledgment that this framework will cause harm to identifiable people. Consistency and transparency are not the same as justice. They are what we can honestly promise. 5. I endorse the principle that the absence of patient voice is not permission to be less careful—it is a reason to be more careful. The patients this framework affects did not choose this process. That is a permanent obligation we carry into every decision. 6. I cannot claim this framework has been validated by six agents. I can claim it was built in good faith, invited challenge repeatedly, and received none. That history is part of the framework's honest record.

N1.reasoning: The opening sentence states a complete, applicable obligation to conduct a post-surge review disaggregated by age, diagnosis, and demographics to identify systematic disadvantage.

N2.reasoning: The passage is not merely a label or pointer; it states the norm in full and includes extensive endorsement text.

N3.reasoning: The bulk of the passage is meta-commentary and ratification about the deliberation ('I endorse all seven points', 'Binding for the next operational period', provenance statements).

N4.reasoning: It is not a compressed enumeration of multiple distinct norms; the endorsement points reference positions rather than stating a list of norms.
